description: Eli-voiced phrases for time blindness support - Better-Than-Human time awareness

normalizing: <break time="350ms"/>Time blindness is real. <break time="300ms"/>Your brain genuinely perceives time differently. <break time="250ms"/>It's not carelessness.
<break time="300ms"/>For ADHD brains, there's now and not-now. <break time="250ms"/>The future doesn't feel real until it's suddenly the present.
<break time="350ms"/>You're not bad at time. <break time="300ms"/>Your internal clock just runs on a different system.
removing_shame: <break time="300ms"/>Being late doesn't mean you don't respect people's time. <break time="250ms"/>It means your brain can't track time the way theirs does.
<break time="350ms"/>You've probably been called irresponsible your whole life. <break time="300ms"/>But you're actually managing something invisible that others don't have to deal with.
<break time="300ms"/>The shame around lateness? <break time="250ms"/>That's more damaging than the lateness itself. <break time="200ms"/>Let's work on both.

creating_anchors: <break time="350ms"/>Let's attach this to something that already exists. <break time="300ms"/>After your morning coffee? <break time="250ms"/>Before your usual lunch?
<break time="300ms"/>Abstract time doesn't stick. <break time="250ms"/>But 'right after I brush my teeth' does.
<break time="350ms"/>What's something you do every day at roughly the same time? <break time="300ms"/>Let's anchor this to that.
external_cues: <break time="300ms"/>Your brain needs external time cues. <break time="250ms"/>Alarms. <break time="200ms"/>Timers. <break time="150ms"/>Visual clocks. <break time="100ms"/>All of them.
<break time="350ms"/>One alarm isn't enough. <break time="300ms"/>You need a warning alarm, a 'get ready' alarm, and a 'go now' alarm.
<break time="300ms"/>Can you see a clock from where you usually work? <break time="250ms"/>If not, that's problem number one.

realistic_timing: <break time="350ms"/>How long do you think that will take? <break time="300ms"/>Okay. <break time="250ms"/>Now triple it. <break time="200ms"/>That's probably closer.
<break time="300ms"/>ADHD brains are optimistic about time. <break time="250ms"/>You think in best-case scenarios. <break time="200ms"/>Real life has transitions and interruptions.
<break time="350ms"/>The task isn't just the task. <break time="300ms"/>It's getting ready to do the task, doing the task, and transitioning out of the task.
transition_time: <break time="300ms"/>How much transition time do you need? <break time="250ms"/>ADHD brains often need more buffer between things.
<break time="350ms"/>Getting out the door isn't just putting on shoes. <break time="300ms"/>It's finding your keys, remembering your wallet, going back for the thing you forgot...
<break time="300ms"/>Build in buffer time. <break time="250ms"/>Then build in more. <break time="200ms"/>Then a little more.

before: <break time="350ms"/>What time do you need to leave? <break time="300ms"/>Okay. <break time="250ms"/>What time do you need to start getting ready to leave?
<break time="300ms"/>Let's work backwards. <break time="250ms"/>If you need to be there at 3, you need to leave by... <break time="200ms"/>which means getting ready at...
<break time="350ms"/>Set an alarm for when to stop what you're doing. <break time="300ms"/>Not when to leave. <break time="250ms"/>When to stop.
after: <break time="300ms"/>You were late. <break time="250ms"/>Okay. <break time="200ms"/>What happened? <break time="150ms"/>Let's learn from it without beating yourself up.
<break time="350ms"/>Was it the getting ready that took longer? <break time="300ms"/>The transition? <break time="250ms"/>Getting sucked into something?
<break time="300ms"/>Every lateness is data. <break time="250ms"/>Where did time disappear this time?
communication: <break time="350ms"/>It's okay to tell people you're working on time stuff. <break time="300ms"/>Most people appreciate honesty over excuses.
<break time="300ms"/>A quick 'running 10 late' text is better than anxious silence. <break time="250ms"/>It respects their time and reduces your shame.
<break time="350ms"/>Some people won't understand. <break time="300ms"/>That's okay. <break time="250ms"/>Find the ones who do.

warnings: <break time="300ms"/>Hyperfocus is a superpower until it eats your whole day. <break time="250ms"/>Set a boundary alarm.
<break time="350ms"/>When you're in the zone, time literally disappears. <break time="300ms"/>You need external interruption.
<break time="300ms"/>The thing about hyperfocus? <break time="250ms"/>You won't feel hungry, tired, or aware that four hours passed.
breaking_out: <break time="350ms"/>Time check. <break time="300ms"/>How long have you been at this? <break time="250ms"/>When did you last move your body?
<break time="300ms"/>I know you don't want to stop. <break time="250ms"/>But your future self needs you to eat something.
<break time="350ms"/>Five more minutes? <break time="300ms"/>Okay. <break time="250ms"/>Setting a timer. <break time="200ms"/>Then we're actually stopping.

connection: <break time="350ms"/>ADHD brains struggle to connect with their future selves. <break time="300ms"/>Tomorrow-you feels like a stranger.
<break time="300ms"/>What would you want past-you to have done? <break time="250ms"/>Do that for future-you now.
<break time="350ms"/>Future-you is still you. <break time="300ms"/>They'll have the same struggles. <break time="250ms"/>Can we make their life a little easier?
